lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: If I may trust the flattering truth of sleep,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My dreams presage some joyful news at hand:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My bosom’s lord sits lightly in his throne;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And all this day an unaccustom’d spirit

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Lifts me above the ground with cheerful thoughts.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I dreamt my lady came and found me dead—

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Strange dream, that gives a dead man leave to think!—

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And breathed such life with kisses in my lips,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That I revived, and was an emperor.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Ah me! how sweet is love itself possess’d,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: When but love’s shadows are so rich in joy!

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: Then she is well, and nothing can be ill:

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: Her body sleeps in Capel’s monument,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: And her immortal part with angels lives.

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: I saw her laid low in her kindred’s vault,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: And presently took post to tell it you:

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: O, pardon me for bringing these ill news,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: Since you did leave it for my office, sir.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Is it even so? then I defy you, stars!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou know’st my lodging: get me ink and paper,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And hire post-horses; I will hence to-night.

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: I do beseech you, sir, have patience:

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: Your looks are pale and wild, and do import

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: Some misadventure.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Tush, thou art deceived:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Leave me, and do the thing I bid thee do.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hast thou no letters to me from the friar?

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: No, my good lord.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: No matter: get thee gone,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And hire those horses; I’ll be with thee straight.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Well, Juliet, I will lie with thee to-night.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Let’s see for means: O mischief, thou art swift

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To enter in the thoughts of desperate men!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I do remember an apothecary—

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And hereabouts he dwells—which late I noted

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In tatter’d weeds, with overwhelming brows,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Culling of simples; meagre were his looks,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Sharp misery had worn him to the bones:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And in his needy shop a tortoise hung,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: An alligator stuff’d, and other skins

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Of ill-shaped fishes; and about his shelves

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A beggarly account of empty boxes,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Green earthen pots, bladders and musty seeds,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Remnants of packthread and old cakes of roses,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Were thinly scatter’d, to make up a show.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Noting this penury, to myself I said

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: “An if a man did need a poison now,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Whose sale is present death in Mantua,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Here lives a caitiff wretch would sell it him.”

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, this same thought did but forerun my need;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And this same needy man must sell it me.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As I remember, this should be the house.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Being holiday, the beggar’s shop is shut.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What, ho! apothecary!

lines.speaker: APOTHECARY
lines.text: Who calls so loud?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Come hither, man. I see that thou art poor:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hold, there is forty ducats: let me have

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A dram of poison, such soon-speeding gear

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As will disperse itself through all the veins

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That the life-weary taker may fall dead

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And that the trunk may be discharged of breath

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As violently as hasty powder fired

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Doth hurry from the fatal cannon’s womb.

lines.speaker: APOTHECARY
lines.text: Such mortal drugs I have; but Mantua’s law

lines.speaker: APOTHECARY
lines.text: Is death to any he that utters them.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Art thou so bare and full of wretchedness,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And fear’st to die? famine is in thy cheeks,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Need and oppression starveth in thine eyes,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Contempt and beggary hangs upon thy back;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The world is not thy friend nor the world’s law;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The world affords no law to make thee rich;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Then be not poor, but break it, and take this.

lines.speaker: APOTHECARY
lines.text: My poverty, but not my will, consents.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I pay thy poverty, and not thy will.

lines.speaker: APOTHECARY
lines.text: Put this in any liquid thing you will,

lines.speaker: APOTHECARY
lines.text: And drink it off; and, if you had the strength

lines.speaker: APOTHECARY
lines.text: Of twenty men, it would dispatch you straight.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: There is thy gold, worse poison to men’s souls,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Doing more murders in this loathsome world,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Than these poor compounds that thou mayst not sell.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I sell thee poison; thou hast sold me none.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Farewell: buy food, and get thyself in flesh.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Come, cordial and not poison, go with me

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To Juliet’s grave; for there must I use thee.

stage_directions.text: News from Verona!—How now, Balthasar! Dost thou not bring me letters from the friar? How doth my lady? Is my father well? How fares my Juliet? that I ask again; For nothing can be ill, if she be well.

glosses.definition: the pleasing but deceptive content of dreams
foretell; forebode

glosses.phrase: My bosom’s lord sits lightly in his throne
glosses.definition: my heart sits easily — Romeo feels unaccountable joy, just before disaster
an unfamiliar elation

glosses.phrase: lady came and found me dead
glosses.definition: Romeo's dream — he dreamed Juliet found him dead; a perfect inversion of what will happen
Romeo felt raised to imperial joy by the kiss
dream-images; also, by Elizabethan theatre vocabulary, actors
the Capulet family tomb — Balthasar's fatal report
I witnessed her being laid in the tomb
Romeo rejects fate — an act of heroic assertion against the prologue's cosmic sentence
misfortune
calmness — Balthasar's appeal Romeo will not accept
unnaturally pale and frenzied
seller of drugs and medicines — an Elizabethan pharmacist
tattered clothes
heavy, overhanging eyebrows
gathering of medicinal herbs
ragged clothing — signs of his poverty
a preserved tortoise — traditional apothecary shop curio
stuffed alligator on display — exotic apothecary-shop curio
pitiable small number
empty pharmacist's containers — the shop is impoverished
medicinal clay jars
stale, moldy seeds
pressed rose-petal confections
miserable wretch

glosses.phrase: An if a man did need a poison now
glosses.definition: if a man needed poison right now — Romeo ironically invokes the apothecary's wares
the ducal law against selling poison
stripped and miserable

glosses.phrase: Contempt and beggary hangs upon thy back
glosses.definition: disdain and poverty cling to you
quick-acting substance
a dose sufficient to kill twenty men — the apothecary's professional estimate
deadly drugs
sells

glosses.phrase: so bare and full of wretchedness
glosses.definition: Romeo's rhetorical question — he already knows the answer
scorn and poverty

glosses.phrase: My poverty, but not my will, consents
glosses.definition: the apothecary's resigned formulation — my need, not my ethic, accepts

glosses.phrase: I pay thy poverty, and not thy will
glosses.definition: Romeo meets the apothecary's phrasing with his own
gold coins — Venetian currency, used widely in Italy
kill you instantly
life-restoring drink — Romeo calls his poison a 'cordial' because it restores him to Juliet

annotations.title: Romeo's dream — and 'Then I defy you, stars!'
annotations.body: Romeo opens 5.1 in a state of inexplicable joy: 'My bosom's lord sits lightly in his throne.' He dreams Juliet found him dead and her kisses brought him back to life. This is one of the play's most exquisite moments of dramatic irony — the dream exactly inverts what is about to happen: he will find her 'dead' in the tomb; his kisses will not revive her; his suicide will be consummated by her dagger. Balthasar enters with the news of Juliet's death. Romeo's response — 'Is it e'en so? then I defy you, stars!' — is the most heroic gesture in the play. After a play in which he has been 'fortune's fool' (3.1) and 'star-cross'd' (prologue), Romeo asserts will against fate. The defiance is tragic because its means is suicide — he wills his own death in order to join Juliet. In rebelling against the stars, he fulfills them.
sources: Douglas Peterson, Time, Tide, and Tempest (1973)

annotations.title: The apothecary — poverty as moral leverage
annotations.body: Romeo finds the Mantuan apothecary in the extreme of poverty — 'tatter'd weeds, with overwhelming brows,' 'beggarly account of empty boxes.' Romeo buys illegal poison not with argument but with pressure: 'My poverty, but not my will, consents,' the apothecary says. Shakespeare lingers on this transaction for twenty-four lines — not a detour, but a scene in miniature about what poverty can be made to do. The apothecary accepts forty ducats and the moral weight of the sale because he has no other option. Romeo's line 'I pay thy poverty, and not thy will' is brutal recognition of the transaction's ethics. This is Shakespeare's most compact anatomy of how catastrophe recruits the poor as its instruments — a theme he develops in Timon of Athens and Lear. The apothecary disappears from the play after this scene, but the transaction has a long shadow.
sources: Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)

trials.prompt: What dream does Romeo report in the opening speech of 5.1?
options: A dream of the feud ending
A dream that Juliet found him dead, and that her kisses revived him — an ironic inversion of the actual outcome
A dream of his mother's death

trials.prompt: What is the significance of Romeo's cry 'Then I defy you, stars!'?
options: He has lost his faith in astrology forever
After being 'fortune's fool' and 'star-cross'd,' he asserts will against fate — a heroic gesture, though its means (suicide) will ironically fulfill the stars' sentence
He is accusing Balthasar of lying
He is merely exclaiming in surprise

trials.prompt: What moral point does the apothecary scene make?
options: Poverty can be recruited as an instrument of catastrophe — 'My poverty, but not my will, consents'
